---
title: "Feature Engineering"
format: html # This qmd file will be compiled as an HTML web page
---

**Open the associated notebook**: <a href="https://datalab.sspcloud.fr/launcher/ide/vscode-python?name=Notebook1&version=2.3.19&s3=region-79669f20&init.personalInit=«https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2FAIML4OS%2FAIML4OS-template-quarto-python%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Fsspcloud%2Finit-trainees.sh»&init.personalInitArgs=«notebooks%2Fnotebook1.ipynb»&git.repository=«https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2FAIML4OS%2FAIML4OS-template-quarto-python.git»&autoLaunch=true" target="_blank" rel="noopener" data-original-href="https://datalab.sspcloud.fr/launcher/ide/vscode-python?name=Notebook1&version=2.3.19&s3=region-79669f20&init.personalInit=«https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2FAIML4OS%2FAIML4OS-template-quarto-python%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Fsspcloud%2Finit-trainees.sh»&init.personalInitArgs=«notebooks%2Fnotebook1.ipynb»&git.repository=«https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2FAIML4OS%2FAIML4OS-template-quarto-python.git»&autoLaunch=true"><img src="https://custom-icon-badges.demolab.com/badge/SSP%20Cloud-Launch_with_VSCode-blue?logo=vsc&amp;logoColor=white" alt="Onyxia"/></a>

# Introduction

Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into meaningful input variables (features) that improve the performance and accuracy of predictive models. In the context of forecasting the number of foreign tourists visiting five selected countries, feature engineering helps reveal important patterns, seasonality, trends, and external factors that influence tourism flows.

# Key Aspects of Feature Engineering for Tourism Forecasting

## 1. Creating Time-Based Features

Tourism data is strongly seasonal. Time features help models learn recurring patterns. Examples include:

-   Month, Quarter, Year

-   Day of the week (useful for high-frequency data)

-   Season indicators (e.g., summer/winter tourism peaks)

-   Holiday periods, such as Christmas or national holidays

## 2. Lag Features

These capture how the number of tourists in previous periods influences future values. Examples:

-   Tourists lagged by 1 month

-   Lag 3, lag 6, lag 12 (to capture annual seasonality)

## 3. Rolling Window Statistics

Rolling features smooth fluctuations and describe recent trends. Examples:

-   3-month moving average

-   12-month rolling sum (useful for year-over-year analysis)

-   Rolling standard deviation (volatility of tourism flows)

## 4. Pandemic / Shock Indicators

Tourism is highly sensitive to global shocks. Examples:

-   COVID-19 lockdown periods

-   Political instability indicators

-   Natural disaster events

## 5. Interaction Features

Interactions help models understand combined effects, such as: “Summer season” × “Low exchange rate”.

# Why do we design features?

Well-designed features help:

-   identify seasonal tourism peaks,

-   capture long-term trends,

-   incorporate economic and social factors that influence travel decisions,

-   improve the accuracy of predictive models.

Ultimately, feature engineering enables the model to better understand what drives the number of foreign tourists in each of the five selected countries and to produce more reliable forecasts.

# Performed steps

## Step 1

Import necessary libraries and load the dataset.

```{python}
import pyreadr
data = pyreadr.read_r(r'C:\W9 repo z templatki\WP9_S2_PL_Foreign_Tourists\synthetic data\WP9_S2_Tourism_synt.rds').popitem()[1]


```

## Step 2

Import libraries for feature engineering and create new features based on the original dataset.

```{python}


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np


```

## Step 3

Download the dataset and create a dictionary of dataframes, one for each KKR (country). Then, define a function to add features such as rolling means, lags, and group statistics (mean, standard deviation, coefficient of variation) based on different grouping variables (WON, POW, GMN, RO). Finally, apply this function to each dataframe in the dictionary.

```{python}



# --- Get unique KKR values ---
kkr_list = data["KKR"].unique()

# --- Create a dictionary (equivalent of list in R) ---
tourist_kkr_dict = {
    f"tourist_kkr_{kkr}": data[data["KKR"] == kkr].copy()
    for kkr in kkr_list
}

# --- Function to add features ---
def add_features_to_df(df, tourist_col="D8R1"):
    
    # --- Convert OKRES -> date and sort ---
    df["data"] = pd.to_datetime(df["OKRES"].astype(str).str[:4] + "-" +
                               df["OKRES"].astype(str).str[4:6] + "-01")
    df = df.sort_values("data")
    
    # --- Moving averages: 3, 6, 9 months ---
    df["rollmean_3"] = df[tourist_col].rolling(window=3).mean()
    df["rollmean_6"] = df[tourist_col].rolling(window=6).mean()
    df["rollmean_9"] = df[tourist_col].rolling(window=9).mean()
    
    # --- Lags: 1–12 months ---
    for i in range(1, 13):
        df[f"lag_{i}"] = df[tourist_col].shift(i)
    
    # --- Function to compute coefficient of variation (CV) ---
    def add_group_stats(df, group_col, prefix):
        grouped = df.groupby(group_col)[tourist_col]
        
        mean = grouped.transform("mean")
        std = grouped.transform("std")
        
        df[f"{prefix}_mean"] = mean
        df[f"{prefix}_sd"] = std
        df[f"{prefix}_cv"] = np.where(mean == 0, np.nan, std / mean)
        
        return df

    # --- Aggregations and merging back to rows ---
    df = add_group_stats(df, "WON", "WON")
    df = add_group_stats(df, "POW", "POW")
    df = add_group_stats(df, "GMN", "GMN")
    df = add_group_stats(df, "RO", "RO")

    return df


# --- Overwrite dictionary with enriched dataframes ---
tourist_kkr_dict = {
    key: add_features_to_df(df, tourist_col="D8R1")
    for key, df in tourist_kkr_dict.items()
}

# --- Check keys ---
print(tourist_kkr_dict.keys())
```

## Step 3

Dataset is too large to deal with it in a notebook, so we will save enriched dataframes to separate CSV files for each KKR (country) and use them in the next steps of the project. Thats the reason why we choose only 10 couttries weith the highest number of tourists in the original dataset.. Dataset for each coutry will separate file with name "tourist_kkr\_{KKR}.csv" in the "enriched_data" folder.

```{python}
# --- Create output folder ---
output_dir = "top10_countries_features"
os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

# --- Select top 10 countries ---
top_kkr = (
    data.groupby("KKR")["D8R1"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(10)
    .index
)
```

And at final we will save enriched dataframes to separate CSV files for each KKR (country) and use them in the next steps of the project.

# Save each country separately 

```{python}
import os

# --- Correct relative path ---
output_dir = os.path.join("..", "synthetic data")

# --- Ensure directory exists ---
os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

print("Saving to:", os.path.abspath(output_dir))


for kkr in top_kkr:
    df_kkr = data[data["KKR"] == kkr].copy()
    
    df_kkr = add_features_to_df(df_kkr, tourist_col="D8R1")
    
    safe_kkr = str(kkr).replace(" ", "_")
    file_path = os.path.join(output_dir, f"tourist_{safe_kkr}_features.xlsx")
    
    df_kkr.to_excel(file_path, index=False)

print("Files saved to correct folder!")
```
